reasoning: The FRED data reveals that option-adjusted spreads for B-rated high yield bonds spiked from 2.98 on July 31, 2025, to 3.27 on Friday, August 1, 2025. This abrupt increase was triggered by a dual macro shock: a weak US jobs report indicating a rising unemployment rate, and the announcement of new tariffs by President Trump, leading to a 1.6% drop in the S&P 500. Comparing this to the exact historical analogue of August 2024 (when a weak jobs report on August 2 also caused spreads to spike), spreads typically remain elevated for 1–2 weeks before strongly mean-reverting. By August 10, momentum and high near-term volatility suggest the spread has a good chance of remaining above the August 1 panic level of 3.27. By September 2, historical mean reversion indicates a strong likelihood of dropping back below 3.27, absent a sustained macro deterioration. Over the 3- and 6-month horizons (November 2025, January 2026), uncertainty strictly increases towards a 50/50 toss-up, balancing the possibility of a true recessionary widening against further mean-reversion to pre-August tight levels.
